meta.description: Consolidated Critical+High remediation candidates across all 7 findings files. Audit-only: no fixes applied. Every candidate carries owner, remediation, estimate, sprint candidate.

candidates.summary: If the process crashes or a later save throws after an earlier one has committed (e.g. Opportunity.save at line 176 succeeds but Lead.save at line 177 throws), the result is an orphaned Account/Contact/Opportunity in the database while the
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: File docstring at line 97 states '8. Persists all changes atomically', but the method mutates and saves FOUR separate aggregates via four independent, non-transactional repository calls with no shared transaction: Account.save at line 252 (inside handleAccount, with its own try/catch at 251-257 and a separate best-effort event publish at 262-268), Contact.save at line 299 (inside createContactFromLead, own try/catch 298-304, own best-effort publish 309-315), and finally Opportunity.save + Lead.save together at lines 176-177 inside a third, later try/catch (175-182). `grep -n "transaction\|\$transaction\|withTransaction" packages/application/src/usecases/leads/ConvertLeadToDealUseCase.ts` returns zero matches — there is no transaction boundary anywhere in the file. The same non-atomic pattern (Account.save, then later Contact.save + Lead.save, as two separate try/catch blocks) is independently duplicated in packages/application/src/services/LeadService.ts convertLead() at lines 344-358 (account) and 386-392 (contact+lead).
candidates.remediation: Either (a) wrap the whole conversion in a single packages/db withTransaction call so all four aggregate writes commit or roll back together (requires each Prisma*Repository to accept an optional TransactionClient), or (b) redesign as a saga: persist Lead-conversion-intent first, then create Account/Contact/Opportunity idempotently with compensating deletes on failure, and only flip Lead to CONVERTED after all downstream aggregates exist. Do not claim atomicity in the docstring until one of these is implemented.

candidates.summary: A complete, presumably-tested hexagonal implementation for tickets (port + 2 adapters) is dead code that never runs in production. The actual runtime behavior is governed entirely by TicketService's ad-hoc Prisma queries, so any bug fix or
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "TicketRepository" apps/api/src/container.ts` returns 0 matches — container.ts never imports or instantiates PrismaTicketRepository or InMemoryTicketRepository. Yet packages/application/src/ports/repositories/TicketRepositoryPort.ts defines the full port contract (re-exported from packages/application/src/ports/repositories/index.ts:13), and packages/adapters/src/repositories/PrismaTicketRepository.ts + InMemoryTicketRepository.ts implement it. `grep -rl "PrismaTicketRepository" apps/` and `grep -rl "TicketRepositoryPort" apps/` both return 0 files under apps/ (only non-code hits in Sprint_plan CSV/YAML metadata). Instead, container.ts:564 does `const ticketService = new TicketService(prismaClient);` — TicketService (apps/api/src/services/TicketService.ts:19) takes a raw PrismaClient in its constructor.
candidates.remediation: Either (a) wire PrismaTicketRepository into container.ts and refactor TicketService to accept TicketRepositoryPort instead of PrismaClient, or (b) if TicketService's raw-Prisma approach is intentionally kept for now, delete the orphaned port/adapter pair and document the decision in an ADR so the dead code doesn't mislead future contributors.

candidates.summary: The list/getById/listByCase/stats/case-linking/attendee-management surface of the single largest router in the legal module (1369 lines, apps/api/src/modules/legal/appointments.router.ts) bypasses the application layer entirely for reads an
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -c "prismaWithTenant" apps/api/src/modules/legal/appointments.router.ts` = 48 occurrences (28 distinct query/mutation call sites) e.g. line 515 `ctx.prismaWithTenant.appointment.findMany(...)`, line 662 `.findMany`, line 681 `.user.findMany`, line 1150-1280 appointmentCase/appointmentAttendee create/delete, line 1296-1337 stats aggregation. Contrast with the SAME file's mutation endpoints which DO route through the container: line 487 `container.scheduleAppointmentUseCase.execute(...)`, line 753 `container.rescheduleAppointmentUseCase.execute(...)`, line 891 `container.completeAppointmentUseCase.execute(...)`, line 923 `container.cancelAppointmentUseCase.execute(...)`, line 1024/1077/1108 `container.checkConflictsUseCase.*`. container.ts:611-614 documents the gap explicitly: 'Router currently talks to AppointmentDomainService + direct Prisma; these use cases are the canonical path for new callers and will replace the direct-Prisma mutations in a follow-up refactor.'
candidates.remediation: Complete the migration: introduce AppointmentQuery use cases (list/getById/listByCase/stats) and case/attendee-link use cases in packages/application/src/usecases/scheduling/, backed by AppointmentRepositoryPort methods, and remove the remaining ctx.prismaWithTenant.appointment.* calls from the router.

candidates.summary: JS falsy-coercion bug (`threshold || 100`) in duplicate-rule-evaluator.ts means any dedup rule configured with threshold=0 ("match everything") silently behaves as threshold=100 ("match only exact"), causing real duplicate leads/contacts/ac
candidates.remediation: Change `threshold || 100` to `threshold ?? 100` in duplicate-rule-evaluator.ts's resolveFloor (referenced at line 166 per the test comment) and un-skip.

candidates.summary: This is a security-relevant sanitizer (command-injection pattern detection + PII redaction for AI prompts/outputs, per packages/adapters/src/shared/prompt-sanitizer.ts and its apps/api counterpart, both carrying eslint-disable annotations).
candidates.remediation: Consolidate into a single source of truth (e.g. packages/validators or a new packages/security shared module), re-export from both current call sites, and add an architecture test (tests/architecture/) asserting no second copy of the sanitizer regex exists.

candidates.summary: Any authenticated user of ANY tenant (auth only requires `tenantProcedure`, i.e. a valid JWT + tenantId claim — no special role) can enumerate contact firstName/lastName/email/company belonging to every OTHER tenant in the system via the em
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `searchContacts: tenantProcedure.input(z.object({ query: z.string(), limit: z.number().default(5) })).query(async ({ input, ctx }) => { ... const contacts = await (ctx as any).prisma.contact.findMany({ where: { OR: [ { firstName: { contains: input.query, mode: 'insensitive' } }, { lastName: { contains: input.query, mode: 'insensitive' } }, { email: { contains: input.query, mode: 'insensitive' } } ] }, select: { id, firstName, lastName, email, company }, take: input.limit, orderBy: { firstName: 'asc' } }); return contacts; })` at apps/api/src/modules/email/inbound.router.ts:1179-1216. `where` contains only an `OR` array — no `tenantId` key anywhere — and the query runs on the raw `(ctx as any).prisma` client (not `ctx.prismaWithTenant`, which is the only client that sets the RLS session variable — see apps/api/src/trpc.ts:291-327 and apps/api/src/security/tenant-context.ts:119-149). Every sibling procedure in the same router (getEmail L274-280, listEmails L297-298, processEmail L365-369, getThread L503-521, getAttachment L567-573, lookupByEmail L992-997/1026-1027, listTemplates L793-794, markAsRead/markAsUnread L827/853, setLabels L886-891, getStorageUsage L1071, getRelatedMessages L1138-1144) explicitly threads `tenantId` into `where`, confirming this is the one procedure where it was omitted, not an intentional global-search feature.
candidates.remediation: Add `tenantId` to the `where` clause (`where: { tenantId, OR: [...] }`) using `(ctx as any).tenant.tenantId` exactly as the other procedures in this file do, OR switch the query to `ctx.prismaWithTenant.contact.findMany(...)` so RLS session context is also set as a second layer. Add a regression test asserting cross-tenant contacts are never returned. Recommend a repo-wide grep audit (`ctx.prisma.<model>.find*` without `tenantId` in the same call) as a follow-up task, and consider an ESLint rule or Prisma extension that fails the build on tenant-model queries missing tenantId.

candidates.summary: CLAUDE.md is the root Intent Node read at the start of every session per its own 'Session Continuation' rule; a stale sprint/task-count anchor at the very top of the file can mislead any agent or human skimming it before reading SESSION_CON
candidates.evidence: CLAUDE.md:3 reads: '**IntelliFlow CRM** — AI-powered CRM system. Sprint 6 (MVP phase). IFC-010 Go/No-Go passed 2025-12-27. 316 tasks across 34 sprints tracked in `Sprint_plan.csv`.' Reproducible: `head -5 CLAUDE.md`. The actual current sprint is 18-19 per docs/SESSION_CONTEXT.md:7 ('Active sprint: **Sprint 18** (sprint-18) — 94% complete') and the CSV now has 597 rows (`python3 -c "import csv;print(len(list(csv.DictReader(open('apps/project-tracker/docs/metrics/_global/Sprint_plan.csv')))))"` -> 597), not 316.
candidates.remediation: Update CLAUDE.md line 3 to reference the current sprint dynamically or drop the hardcoded sprint/task-count entirely in favor of pointing to docs/SESSION_CONTEXT.md (which is already regenerated and already linked lower in the same file under 'Session Continuation').

candidates.summary: Any tool or agent that reads _summary.json instead of Sprint_plan.csv/SESSION_CONTEXT.md for Sprint 18 status would report the sprint as 7% complete instead of 94% complete — a 87-point error that could trigger false blocker escalation or d
candidates.evidence: File reads: `"generated_at": "2026-04-11T16:48:01.958Z"`, `"task_summary": {"total": 43, "completed": 3, "backlog": 40, "blocked": 0, "attested_completed": 3}`. Reproducible: `cat .specify/sprints/sprint-18/_summary.json | head -12`. This contradicts the CSV-verified truth (63 of 67 Target-Sprint-18 rows are Status=Completed, computed via `python3 csv.DictReader` filter above) and contradicts docs/SESSION_CONTEXT.md:7 ('Sprint 18 ... 94% complete (63/67 tasks)', regenerated 2026-07-22T00:09:10Z, i.e. same day as this audit).
candidates.remediation: Either wire _summary.json regeneration into the same generator that produces docs/SESSION_CONTEXT.md/CURRENT_STATE_REPORT.md, or delete it and point consumers at the actively-maintained reports.

candidates.summary: Violates ADR-011's core promise ('Zero Lost Events... Events stored atomically with domain state changes... No dual-write problem'). If the process crashes, times out, or the outbox write fails between the aggregate save and the event publi
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 69-84 — `await this.leadRepository.save(lead)` (a full, separately-committed Prisma write) completes and returns BEFORE `await this.eventBus.publishAll(events)` is invoked (lines 78-83), and any publish failure is caught and only `console.error`'d, never surfaced or retried: `catch (error) { console.error('Failed to publish domain events:', error); }`. The identical pattern (save, then separate best-effort publishAll wrapped in try/catch that only logs) repeats in packages/application/src/services/LeadService.ts publishEvents() at lines 698-708, and in ConvertLeadToDealUseCase.ts publishEvents() at lines 370-387. Even though packages/adapters/src/events/OutboxEventBusAdapter.ts publishAll() (lines 110-186) itself uses `withTransaction` for the outbox-table INSERTs, that transaction is a separate DB transaction from the one (if any) used by PrismaLeadRepository.save().
candidates.remediation: Extend LeadRepository.save (and sibling repositories) to accept an optional TransactionClient, and have each use case open one transaction that both persists the aggregate and writes the outbox rows, e.g. `withTransaction(tx => { leadRepo.save(lead, tx); outbox.publishAll(events, tx); })`. Remove the swallow-and-log catch blocks once this is atomic — a failure should fail the whole use case.

candidates.summary: TenantId is the single most safety-critical field in a multi-tenant SaaS domain (the isolation boundary between customers), yet it has zero domain-level validation (format, non-empty, non-placeholder) and can be silently swapped for an empt
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `tenantId: string;` appears as a raw, unvalidated primitive in every aggregate's props interface — Lead.ts:65, packages/domain/src/crm/contact/Contact.ts:81, packages/domain/src/crm/account/Account.ts:71, packages/domain/src/crm/opportunity/Opportunity.ts:74 (163 occurrences of the literal `tenantId: string` across 49 files per `grep -rn "tenantId: string" packages/domain/src | wc -l`). `find packages/domain/src -iname "*tenant*"` returns only CrossTenantOrNotFoundError.ts (an error type, not a value object) — no TenantId class exists anywhere in packages/domain/src/shared or elsewhere, unlike Email, PhoneNumber, Money, Percentage, WebsiteUrl which all have dedicated Value Objects with Result-returning factories.
candidates.remediation: Introduce a TenantId value object in packages/domain/src/shared/TenantId.ts (mirroring Email/PhoneNumber's Result-returning static create()), and incrementally migrate aggregate props from `tenantId: string` to `tenantId: TenantId`, starting with the highest-risk aggregates (Lead, Contact, Account, Opportunity, Invoice). This is a larger multi-sprint migration; recommend landing the VO + Lead first as a template.

candidates.summary: The 'automatically (dis)qualify based on AI score' business rule is a domain policy but is not reachable from the Lead aggregate itself — any other caller that legitimately updates a lead's score (a different use case, a batch job, a future
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: LEAD_SCORE_THRESHOLDS (HOT: 80, WARM: 50, AUTO_QUALIFY: 75, AUTO_DISQUALIFY: 20) is defined as an application-layer constant at lines 22-28. The auto-qualification POLICY itself — 'if a fresh AI score crosses a threshold, automatically call lead.qualify() or lead.changeStatus()' — is implemented as raw if/else in updateLeadScore() at lines 227-244: `if (score >= LEAD_SCORE_THRESHOLDS.AUTO_QUALIFY && lead.status === 'NEW') { lead.qualify(...) } else if (score <= LEAD_SCORE_THRESHOLDS.AUTO_DISQUALIFY && lead.status === 'NEW') { lead.changeStatus('UNQUALIFIED', ...) }`. The Lead aggregate's own qualify()/changeStatus() methods (Lead.ts:334-349, 320-332) only validate state-transition legality (not-already-converted, correct source status) — they have no concept of 'should I auto-qualify based on score', so this policy only fires for callers that happen to go through LeadService.updateLeadScore().
candidates.remediation: Move the auto-qualify/auto-disqualify decision into the Lead aggregate (e.g. a `Lead.applyAiScore(score, confidence, modelVersion, thresholds)` method that internally calls updateScore + conditionally qualify/changeStatus and emits a single coherent event), or at minimum extract a pure domain policy function co-located with LeadScore so every caller shares the same thresholds. Reconcile the 75/20 auto-qualify bounds with LeadScore.tier's 80/50 tier bounds or document why they intentionally differ.

candidates.summary: 18 tasks CSV-marked Completed in Sprint 18 (13 Module-Settings pages PG-196..209 + 5 Terraform INFRA-TF-001..005) shipped without a canonical attestation.json. The code exists (pages verified under apps/web/src/app), but there is no gate/KP
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `for id in PG-196 PG-197 PG-198 PG-199 PG-201..PG-209 INFRA-TF-001..005; do test -f .specify/sprints/sprint-18/attestations/$id/attestation.json; done` -> all 18 MISSING (only task-tracking.json stub present). Triple-corroborated: task-reconciliation.csv (18 Completed-without-json, all Sprint 18), stale-artifacts.json STALE-003, and docs/CURRENT_STATE_REPORT.md.
candidates.remediation: Retro-attest the 18 tasks: run the attestation ceremony (or a documented backfill with git_commit + evidence links) against the already-shipped code. Do NOT delete the code — it exists and passes typecheck/lint/build. This is an evidence backfill, not a re-implementation.

candidates.summary: 44% of completed Sprints 1-18 work cannot be independently re-verified from its attestation alone. Most (159) are pre-ADR-068 attestations lacking git_commit/artifact_hashes; this is historical debt, not fabrication, but it means the 'deliv
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: reconcile.mjs over 408 S1-18 tasks -> Completed=404; with attestation.json=386; with FULL provenance (run_id+timestamp+verdict+artifact_hashes|gate_results)=227; => 177 Completed tasks (44%) lack full provenance (159 have an attestation.json with incomplete provenance + 18 have none).
candidates.remediation: Accept pre-ADR-068 attestations as grandfathered but record the exact 177-task list in the debt ledger; enforce full provenance going forward (already the ADR-068 default). Optionally backfill git_commit for the 17 present-but-incomplete Sprint-18 attestations (cheap, high-value).

candidates.summary: 175 of 447 committed JSON artifacts fail their own JSON-schema validation. The validate:schemas gate is RED on origin/main. Downstream consumers (dashboard, metrics APIs, plan-lint) may be reading structurally-invalid data; schema/data drif
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `pnpm validate:schemas` -> exit 1, '=== Summary === Passed: 272 Failed: 175 Total: 447'. Raw log saved at tool-runs/validate-schemas.log.
candidates.remediation: Triage the 175 failures (bucket by schema); regenerate schemas where structure legitimately changed, fix artifacts where they drifted. Then wire validate:schemas into pre-ship as a hard gate. Separate remediation PR — NOT this audit PR.

candidates.summary: Business/orchestration logic for the entire ticketing feature (SLA calculation, status transitions, assignee resolution — see getAssigneeProfiles at TicketService.ts:41) is untestable without a real/mocked Prisma client and cannot be reused
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: apps/api/src/services/TicketService.ts:1-19 imports `type { PrismaClient, Ticket, TicketStatus, TicketPriority, SLAStatus } from '@intelliflow/db'` and declares `export class TicketService { constructor(private readonly prisma: PrismaClient) {} }`. This class lives under apps/api/src/services/ (API/driving-adapter layer per ADR-047's package structure), not packages/application/src/usecases/, and is coupled directly to Prisma rather than to TicketRepositoryPort (see HEX-001).
candidates.remediation: Migrate TicketService's orchestration logic into a packages/application/src/usecases/TicketUseCases.ts consuming TicketRepositoryPort, with TicketService (if kept in apps/api) reduced to a thin adapter that just forwards to the use case.

candidates.summary: Same class of issue as HEX-002: ticket-routing business rules are directly coupled to Prisma inside the API tier with no port abstraction, so routing logic cannot be unit-tested without a database and cannot be swapped/mocked cleanly.
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "class TicketRoutingService" apps/api/src/services/TicketRoutingService.ts` matches; container.ts:567 does `const ticketRoutingService = new TicketRoutingService(prismaClient);` passing the raw PrismaClient (same `prismaClient: PrismaClient` parameter used for `createServices(prismaClient: PrismaClient)` at container.ts:523). No TicketRoutingRepositoryPort exists in packages/application/src/ports/repositories/.
candidates.remediation: Same remediation family as HEX-002: extract to packages/application/src/usecases/ behind a port, or explicitly document apps/api/src/services/ as an accepted secondary application layer in an ADR if the team intends to keep this pattern.

candidates.summary: Inconsistent architecture within the same bounded context (leads): one code path (LeadService) is properly hexagonal, another (LeadRoutingService, used for IFC-030 lead routing) is not. This makes it harder for contributors to know which pa
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: container.ts:570 does `const leadRoutingService = new LeadRoutingService(prismaClient);`. `ls apps/api/src/services/` confirms LeadRoutingService.ts is co-located with TicketService.ts and TicketRoutingService.ts under apps/api/src/services/, the same raw-PrismaClient-constructor pattern as HEX-002/HEX-003. Note: this is distinct from the properly-wired `leadRepository = new PrismaLeadRepository(prismaClient)` (container.ts:306) used by LeadService — the lead module has BOTH a correct hexagonal path (LeadService via LeadRepositoryPort) and a bypass path (LeadRoutingService via raw Prisma) coexisting.
candidates.remediation: Refactor LeadRoutingService to depend on LeadRepositoryPort (already defined and wired) instead of a raw PrismaClient, reusing the existing PrismaLeadRepository adapter.

candidates.summary: Every free-text search (global search, and the `search` param on each entity's list endpoint) performs a full sequential scan of the tenant's rows in that table today; this scales linearly with tenant data volume. Under ADR-018's k6 budget
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -rn "CREATE EXTENSION" infra/supabase/migrations/*.sql` returns only `uuid-ossp` (20250101000000_initial_schema.sql:9) and `vector` (20250101000000_initial_schema.sql:10) — no `pg_trgm`. Every text-search filter in the app uses Prisma `{ contains: X, mode: 'insensitive' }`, which Postgres compiles to `column ILIKE '%X%'` (leading wildcard). Confirmed call sites: global-search.router.ts:67-249 (lead/contact/account/opportunity/ticket/task, all `contains`+`insensitive`), apps/api/src/modules/lead/lead.router.ts:129-132 & 1450-1453 (email/firstName/lastName/company), apps/api/src/modules/contact/contact.router.ts:162-166,172,1372-1374,1447-1453,1458 (email/firstName/lastName/title/department), apps/api/src/modules/account/account.router.ts:614-617,622,916-918,923 (name/website/industry/description), apps/api/src/modules/opportunity/opportunity.router.ts:425-426,876-877,1052-1053 (name/description/account.name). A leading-wildcard ILIKE cannot use a standard B-tree index (Postgres btree only accelerates prefix `LIKE 'X%'`); it always requires a sequential scan unless a `pg_trgm` GIN/GiST index exists. Contrast with packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma:1589-1590, which explicitly notes a GIN tsvector index was added for MessageRecord full-text search as a 'reviewer follow-up' — the same treatment was never applied to Lead/Contact/Account/Opportunity/Ticket search.
candidates.remediation: Enable `pg_trgm` in a new Supabase migration (`CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS pg_trgm;`) and add `CREATE INDEX ... USING gin (column gin_trgm_ops)` (raw SQL, since Prisma schema.prisma cannot express trigram GIN natively — same pattern already used for MessageRecord's tsvector GIN per schema.prisma:1590) on Lead(email,firstName,lastName,company), Contact(email,firstName,lastName,title,department), Account(name,website,industry), Opportunity(name,description), and Ticket(subject,description). Re-verify with `EXPLAIN ANALYZE` that the ILIKE predicates switch from Seq Scan to Bitmap Index Scan post-migration.

candidates.summary: Five distinct confirmed bugs in Ticket domain state-machine guards (priority mutation and assign/unassign on terminal ARCHIVED/CLOSED tickets, negative SLA-pause duration) ship un-fixed and un-regression-tested. A ticket that should be immu
candidates.remediation: Either fix the isTerminalStatus() guard usage in Ticket.changePriority/assign/unassign (packages/domain ticket aggregate) and un-skip, or file individual Sprint tasks for each RACE-PURE-10/11/M1 finding and reference the task ID in the skip reason per ADR-054.

candidates.summary: Task aggregate's state machine does not consult a transition table before status changes or assignment, so terminal/invalid task states can still be mutated (e.g. re-assigning a CANCELLED task, completing a task that never started). Silent
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "test.skip(" tests/property/unit/crm/task-domain.prop.test.ts` → lines 271, 284, 302, 309, 315. Titles: 'RACE-PURE-09: changeStatus("ARCHIVED", user) on IN_PROGRESS task must be rejected (transition table not consulted)', 'RACE-PURE-09: complete() on a PENDING task ... must be rejected', 'RACE-PURE-M2: assignToLead()/assignToContact()/assignToOpportunity() on COMPLETED/CANCELLED/ARCHIVED task must be rejected'.
candidates.remediation: Introduce VALID_TASK_TRANSITIONS validation in Task.changeStatus/assignTo* (packages/domain task aggregate) and un-skip these five tests; or file tracked tasks per finding ID.

candidates.summary: Two records that both lack name/company data score 100% identical on the composite field (matching on the empty '|' / '||' skeleton), producing false-positive duplicate flags. In a CRM this can incorrectly merge or block creation of genuine
candidates.remediation: Make the composite field extractor return '' when all sub-components are empty (per the comment's own fix proposal) and un-skip.

candidates.summary: ADR-054's flaky-test policy is unenforced. There is no automated gate preventing engineers from committing `it.skip`/`test.skip` calls, which is precisely how the 21 documented-bug skips in QUAL-001 through QUAL-011 entered the codebase und
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "no-skipped-tests" docs/architecture/adr/ADR-054-property-based-race-condition-testing.md eslint.config.mjs` returns ONE match (the ADR line itself: "commented-out tests are rejected by the `no-skipped-tests` ESLint rule already enforced in CI.") and ZERO matches in eslint.config.mjs. `grep -n "vitest" eslint.config.mjs` and `grep -n "no-disabled-tests\|vitest/" eslint.config.mjs` also return no matches — the repo's flat ESLint config has no vitest-specific plugin or rule at all.
candidates.remediation: Add `eslint-plugin-vitest`'s `no-disabled-tests` (or an equivalent custom rule) to eslint.config.mjs scoped to `**/*.test.ts`, with an allow-list mechanism requiring a linked issue ID in the skip reason string, then reconcile all 21 existing bug-documenting skips (QUAL-001 through QUAL-011) either by fixing the underlying bug or filing tracked tasks.

candidates.summary: packages/adapters/src/erp/sap/client.ts (SAPAdapter, SAPAuthenticationError, SAPConnectionError, SAPRateLimitError, SAPNotFoundError) has zero executed test coverage — all 19 tests covering auth, connection errors, rate limiting, and not-fo
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: verbatim lines 22-24: "// Skip until SAP adapter implementation is complete - IFC-099\n// The current stub implementation doesn't match the test expectations\ndescribe.skip('SAP ERP Adapter', () => {". `grep -c "  it(" tests/integration/connectors/erp.test.ts` = 19 individual test cases wrapped inside this single describe.skip.
candidates.remediation: Verify IFC-099's current status in Sprint_plan.csv; either complete the SAP adapter implementation to match the test contract and un-skip, or formally mark ERP/SAP connector as backlog/deferred in the sprint plan so this isn't silently carried forward.

candidates.summary: If (as is standard) the app's DATABASE_URL role owns the tables it migrated, RLS silently does not apply to ANY query issued over that connection — including `ctx.prismaWithTenant` queries that intentionally `SET app.current_tenant_id`. RLS
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -c "ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY" packages/db/prisma/migrations/20260317000000_baseline/migration.sql` → 69. `grep -c "FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY" packages/db/prisma/migrations/20260317000000_baseline/migration.sql` → 0 (confirmed repo-wide: `grep -rc "FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY" packages/db/prisma/migrations/*/migration.sql` returns 0 for every migration file). Line 4822: `ALTER TABLE "contacts" ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` with policy at line 4832 `CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation_contacts ON "contacts" FOR ALL USING ("tenantId" = get_current_tenant_id());` — never followed by `ALTER TABLE "contacts" FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` anywhere in the migrations tree. Per PostgreSQL semantics, a table's OWNER role is exempt from its own RLS policies unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is also set; the Prisma client connects via a single `DATABASE_URL` role (packages/db/src/client.ts:125: `connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL!`) that is the same role which ran these migrations (i.e. very likely the table owner in a standard Supabase/Prisma setup).
candidates.remediation: Verify which Postgres role `DATABASE_URL` connects as in each environment and whether it owns the migrated tables (`SELECT tableowner FROM pg_tables WHERE tablename='contacts';` vs the connection's `current_user`). If it is the owner (expected), add `ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;` for every tenant-scoped table in a follow-up migration, or provision a separate non-owner application role for query traffic (migrations continue to run as the owner/admin role). Add an integration test that connects as the actual app role and asserts a query without `app.current_tenant_id` set returns zero rows across tenants.

candidates.summary: Any authenticated user regardless of role (USER, SALES_REP, MANAGER — not just ADMIN as documented) can register a webhook source with an attacker-chosen `secret`/`signatureVerifier`/`allowedEvents`, or unregister (disable) an existing legi
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 112-140: `/** Register a new webhook source * Protected endpoint - admin only */ registerSource: protectedProcedure.input(registerSourceSchema).mutation(...)` and lines 142-160 `/** Unregister a webhook source * Protected endpoint - admin only */ unregisterSource: protectedProcedure.input(...).mutation(...)`. Both use `protectedProcedure`, imported at line 18 from `../../trpc`. `protectedProcedure` (apps/api/src/trpc.ts:208) only requires `ctx.user` to exist — it does not check role. The project's own `adminProcedure` export exists at apps/api/src/trpc.ts:256 (`t.procedure.use(isAuthed).use(isAdmin).use(tracingMiddleware)`) and `apps/api/src/middleware/auth.ts:54-72` defines `createAdminMiddleware()` for exactly this purpose, but neither is used here.
candidates.remediation: Replace `protectedProcedure` with `adminProcedure` for both `registerSource` and `unregisterSource`. Add a router test asserting a `USER`/`SALES_REP` role receives `FORBIDDEN`. Note: exploitability may currently be reduced by SEC-009 (the webhook service is instantiated fresh per request rather than a shared singleton) — fix both together and re-verify.

candidates.summary: An unauthenticated external party who knows (or guesses) any tenant user's email domain can POST directly to this tRPC mutation and have IntelliFlow store an arbitrary, attacker-controlled 'received' email — with any `from` address, subject
candidates.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: lines 211-266: `export const inboundEmailRouter = createTRPCRouter({ /** Webhook endpoint for receiving inbound emails * This is a public endpoint that email providers call */ webhook: publicProcedure.input(InboundEmailWebhookSchema).mutation(async ({ input, ctx }) => { ... const { emailId, tenantId } = await storeEmail(parsed, ctx); ... }) })`. `InboundEmailWebhookSchema` (lines 43-65) validates shape only (all fields optional strings) — no HMAC/shared-secret/basic-auth check anywhere in this handler or in `storeEmail`/`resolveTenantForInboundEmail` (lines 1259-1315), which resolves the target tenant purely by matching the caller-supplied `to` address's domain against registered users' email domains and then persists the caller-supplied `from`, `subject`, `html`/`text` body as `status: 'DELIVERED'` (line 1349).
candidates.remediation: Add a shared-secret check (e.g. a `?token=` query param or `X-Inbound-Secret` header compared with `timingSafeEqual` against an env-configured secret, mirroring `packages/webhooks/src/framework.ts`'s `hmacSha256Verify` pattern) before processing the payload; alternatively verify the sending IP against the provider's published inbound-parse IP ranges. Track unauthenticated inbound volume in metrics/alerts. Add an ADR documenting the chosen verification mechanism.

candidates.summary: 63 tasks are marked 'Completed' in the single source of truth (Sprint_plan.csv) but 18 of them (29%) have no independently-verifiable evidence file — the CSV's completion claim for those 18 tasks currently rests on the row's own Status colu
candidates.evidence: 18 of 63 CSV-Completed Sprint-18 tasks have an attestation subdirectory but no attestation.json inside it: INFRA-TF-001..005, PG-196..199, PG-201..209. Reproducible: `for d in .specify/sprints/sprint-18/attestations/*/; do id=$(basename "$d"); [ -f "${d}attestation.json" ] || echo "MISSING:$id"; done` then intersect with `python3 -c "import csv; rows=list(csv.DictReader(open('apps/project-tracker/docs/metrics/_global/Sprint_plan.csv'))); print([r['Task ID'] for r in rows if r['Target Sprint']=='18' and r['Status']=='Completed'])"`. Independently corroborated by docs/CURRENT_STATE_REPORT.md line 11 (`CSV marks these tasks completed without a canonical attestation: ...`), which lists all 18 IDs.
candidates.remediation: Run /fix-incomplete-tasks or a dedicated reconcile-and-attest pass over the 18 listed IDs to either produce evidence-backed attestation.json files or flip the CSV Status back if the work cannot be verified.

candidates.summary: An agent following CLAUDE.md's explicit instruction 'DO NOT read Sprint_plan.csv directly ... Use split files' has no way to discover Sprint_plan_J.csv's existence from the documentation alone, and would silently miss the 8 tasks it contain
candidates.evidence: CLAUDE.md:34-43 documents Sprint_plan splits A through I only (the tree diagram terminates at `└── Sprint_plan_I.csv # 26 rows (IFC-297 → IFC-308)`), but `ls apps/project-tracker/docs/metrics/_global/ | grep Sprint_plan` shows a 10th file, Sprint_plan_J.csv, exists on disk (8 rows: PG-210, IFC-314, AUTOMATION-003, ... verified via `wc -l` and `head -2`/`tail -2`). J is not mentioned anywhere in CLAUDE.md.
candidates.remediation: Regenerate the CLAUDE.md split-file table (rows/ranges for A-J) immediately after any `split-sprint-plan.ts` run, ideally via a small script rather than hand-editing, given how often it has already drifted (see STALE-005).

candidates.summary: The README, which is the documented entry point ('ADR Index') for discovering architectural decisions, is missing roughly a fifth of all real ADRs, including recent, high-relevance ones (ADR-064 Terraform SSOT is indexed at line 251, but AD
candidates.evidence: 15 real ADR files exist on disk but appear nowhere in README.md's ADR Index tables: ADR-049-agent-plan-reflect-phases.md, ADR-050-duplicate-detection-runtime.md, ADR-051-public-product-tour.md, ADR-052-redis-monitoring-snapshot-bridge.md, ADR-053-n-plus-one-query-budget-detector.md, ADR-054-property-based-race-condition-testing.md, ADR-056-audit-hash-chain-concurrency.md, ADR-057-fast-pr-vs-full-regression-split.md, ADR-058-vitest-20-shard-strategy.md, ADR-059-ci-cache-strategy.md, ADR-060-required-checks-policy.md, ADR-061-test-impact-changed-test-policy.md, ADR-067-metrics-harness-dechurn-and-canonical-split.md, ADR-068-attestation-provenance-standardization.md. Reproducible: `for n in 049 050 051 052 053 054 056 057 058 059 060 061 067 068; do grep -c "ADR-$n" docs/architecture/adr/README.md; done` returns 0 for every one (all files themselves exist under `ls docs/architecture/adr/ADR-0{49..61}*.md docs/architecture/adr/ADR-06{7,8}*.md`).
candidates.remediation: Add a 'Sprint 16-18 ADRs' (or similarly named) table entry for ADR-049 through ADR-061, ADR-067, and ADR-068, following the same backfill pattern used for the four existing batch tables.
